text: How many days per week do you typically consume political content (news, podcasts, social media, discussions)?

options.label: Rarely or never (less than once a week)
1-2 days per week
5-6 days per week
3-4 days per week
Every day
Movement: Being part of something bigger—collective action, shared vision
Policy: Achieving specific outcomes—I care about results, not rhetoric
Resistance: Fighting against threats—powerful interests, bad actors
Duty: Civic responsibility—participating is important regardless of outcome
Independence: Thinking for myself—I distrust establishments and movements alike

text: Which policy bundle would you prefer?

options.label: Minimal government surveillance
Strong local police presence and enforcement
Expedited trials for serious crimes
Expanded digital surveillance and monitoring
Community-based alternatives to policing
Full due process protections even if slower

text: To win an election, is it better to:

options.label: Dilute the message to build a broader coalition with people you disagree with.
Maintain a clear, principled message, even if it alienates moderate voters.

text: A fixed budget can either save 10 lives domestically or 100 lives abroad. What should it do?
When politics comes up at social gatherings, you typically:

options.label: Share your views but try to keep it civil
Avoid entirely—politics ruins social situations
Try to change the subject—not the place
Engage passionately—these conversations matter
Listen mostly, share selectively

text: Which approach to climate and energy do you prefer?

options.label: Aggressive emissions reductions now
Accept short-term economic disruption for green transition
Higher costs acceptable for clean energy
Gradual transition as technology improves
Protect existing energy jobs
Keep energy affordable for families
Carbon tax letting market find solutions
All low-carbon sources including nuclear
Costs passed to consumers (polluter pays)
Direct regulation and mandates
Renewables only (solar, wind)
Subsidized transition, government absorbs cost

text: You have 30 minutes to read one article in the New York Times. Which headline do you click?

options.label: The proven principles that have guided successful societies for centuries
A bizarre new scientific theory that upends everything we know about physics
Ten practical tips to organize your home and life
Why the other political side is even worse than you thought

text: What do you think the current top marginal federal income tax rate is?
What should the top marginal federal income tax rate be?
A prestigious university is deciding how to allocate limited spots. Which approach do you support?

options.label: Merit plus consideration for economic disadvantage
Lottery among all qualified applicants
Quotas to ensure proportional representation of all groups
Purely by academic merit (test scores, grades)

text: What portion of economic inequality is caused by each? (Must sum to 100%)
Which criminal justice approach do you prefer?

options.label: Focus on rehabilitation and reform
Reduce incarceration/alternatives to prison
Address root causes (poverty, addiction, mental health)
Focus on punishment and deterrence
Longer sentences for violent crime
Protect society by removing criminals from streets
Heavy funding for prevention programs
Shorter sentences with early release options
Restorative justice, victim compensation focus
Balanced funding across system
Mandatory minimums, truth in sentencing
Incapacitation focus (keep offenders away)

text: What's the maximum acceptable ratio between CEO pay and average worker pay?

options.label: 10:1 or less
300:1 (about current average)
No limit - the market should decide

text: How do you view human progress over time?

options.label: Humanity is generally progressing - things get better over time
History moves in cycles - neither improving nor declining overall
Progress is mostly an illusion - human nature doesn't change

text: When bad things happen in politics or the economy, what portion of the blame belongs to each cause? (Must sum to 100%)
A controversial speaker is invited to a university. They have views many find deeply offensive but haven't called for violence. What should happen?

options.label: Cancel the event - some views shouldn't be platformed
Allow but with some restrictions on content
Allow but also provide platform for counter-speech
Allow them to speak with no restrictions

text: Give your best estimate for each, along with your confidence:
A new law will significantly affect your community. Rank who should have the MOST say in shaping it (drag to reorder, 1 = most influence):
Which trait is more important to teach children?
In criminal trials, which type of error is worse?

options.label: Convicting an innocent person
Letting a guilty person go free
Both roughly equally bad

text: For your next vacation, which would you prefer?

options.label: Visit somewhere completely new and unfamiliar
Return to a favorite destination you know well

text: In welfare programs, which error is worse?

options.label: Denying benefits to someone who genuinely needs them
Giving benefits to someone who doesn't qualify
Both roughly equally bad

text: Think back to the COVID-19 pandemic. Assuming you could confirm indoor mask use reduced spread of the virus moderately, how acceptable is a mask mandate for indoor public spaces during a surge?

options.label: Mixed feelings—understand the goal but dislike the mandate
Strongly oppose any mandate—no authority should mandate personal choices
Fully support—public health requires collective action
Resent as overreach—even if masks help
Accept—reasonable given the circumstances

text: A major factory in your region announces it's closing, eliminating 2,000 jobs. Rank these causes from most to least responsible:
Information Control: Platforms and governments must balance two risks. Which error is worse?

options.label: Empowering authorities to decide what is true, potentially silencing dissent
Allowing disinformation to spread, causing harm
Both roughly equally bad

text: Looking at the last 50 years of global trade, countries that significantly opened their economies generally...

options.label: Saw mixed results - some sectors and groups gained, others lost
Often stagnated or fell behind more protectionist countries
Grew wealthier, often at trading partners' expense
Results varied too much by country to generalize
Grew wealthier, as did their trading partners

text: Combining the Wall Street Journal, Bloomberg, and New York Times: what percentage of their factual claims do you believe are accurate?
Immigration Enforcement: Immigration enforcement can be stricter or looser. Which error is worse?

options.label: Deporting someone who is legally eligible to stay
Allowing someone to stay who should be deported
Equally bad

text: Where do the biggest threats to America come from?

options.label: External - foreign powers, terrorism, immigration
Both roughly equally
Internal - domestic extremism, inequality, institutional decay

text: What percentage of the people comprising each group do you think...
Medical Regulation: The FDA must balance speed vs. safety in approving new treatments. Which error is worse?

options.label: Blocking a treatment that would have helped patients (FDA too slow)
Approving a treatment that causes harm (FDA too fast)
Equally bad

text: Your workplace implements a new rule everyone agrees is stupid but technically required by policy. What do you do?

options.label: Follow it - rules are rules
Quietly ignore when possible
Follow while working through proper channels to change it
Malicious compliance to expose the absurdity

text: What portion of people who vote for the party you oppose fall into each category? (Must sum to 100%)
Electoral Security: Election rules can prioritize access or security. Which error is worse?

options.label: Preventing a legitimate voter from voting (disenfranchisement)
Allowing an illegitimate vote to count (voter fraud)
Both roughly equally bad

text: What percentage of your close friends voted differently from you in the last presidential election?

options.label: 0% - None of them
Around 10-25%
Around 25-50%
More than 50%
I don't know how most of them voted

text: If you didn't know what position you would occupy (rich or poor, lucky or unlucky), which society would you choose to live in?

options.label: High (20:1 ratio)
Moderate (3:1 ratio)
Very Low (1.2:1 ratio)

text: How much do you think your political views have changed in the past 10 years?
Which has changed more minds throughout history?

options.label: Logical arguments and empirical evidence
Compelling narratives and storytelling
Religious and spiritual experiences
Economic necessity and material conditions
Art, music, and cultural expression

text: Emotional availability of primary caregivers growing up:
When your political views conflict with those of close friends or family...

options.label: I engage in discussion but stay respectful
I usually keep quiet to avoid conflict
I argue my position strongly—truth matters more
I'll mention my view but won't argue

text: Social progress achieved in the last century is:

options.label: Mostly illusory—superficial changes hiding deeper problems
Fragile—could easily be reversed
Solid—likely to continue expanding
Real but contested—requires constant defense
Permanent—these gains are essentially irreversible

text: Which of the following should be expected of immigrants for proper integration into American society? (Select all that apply)

options.label: Learn to speak English
Learn American history and civics
Adhere to core American values (democracy, freedom, rule of law)
Follow American laws
Work and contribute economically
Adopt American customs and traditions
Identify primarily as American
Abandon previous cultural practices
None of the above—integration should not be expected

text: When you think about what makes someone "truly American," which matters most?

options.label: Sharing core American values and beliefs (freedom, democracy, opportunity)
Being born and raised in America
Having legal citizenship status
Adopting American culture, customs, and way of life
None of these—anyone can be truly American regardless

text: How would you describe your parents' or guardians' political views when you were growing up?

options.label: Strongly liberal/progressive
Somewhat liberal/progressive
Mixed or moderate
Apolitical—politics was rarely discussed
Somewhat conservative
Strongly conservative

text: How would you describe the role of religion in your upbringing?

options.label: Secular—raised without religion or with explicit non-religious worldview
Minimal—rarely attended services or discussed religion
Moderate—some religious exposure but not a major influence
Important—regular practice and religious identity, but not all-encompassing
Central—religion shaped daily life, values, and community

text: Think of times you changed your mind on a significant political issue in the past couple of years. What caused the change? (Check all that apply)

options.label: Read a study, article, or book with new data
Conversation with someone who disagreed with me
Personal experience that contradicted my view
Realized my position conflicted with my deeper values
Saw trusted figures in my community change their view
I can't recall changing my mind on anything significant

text: The most effective political leaders are those who:

options.label: Channel popular anger and frustration into political energy
Paint a compelling vision of what the country could become
Fight hard and win—results matter more than style
Master policy details and explain complex tradeoffs clearly
Build expert coalitions and institutional support

text: How politically engaged were your parents or guardians when you were growing up?

options.label: Somewhat engaged—they voted and occasionally discussed politics
Minimally engaged—they rarely discussed politics or participated
Very engaged—politics was frequently discussed, they voted regularly, may have been activists
Not at all—politics was essentially absent from my upbringing

text: During your childhood and teenage years, how safe did you feel in your neighborhood?

options.label: Mostly safe—occasional concerns but generally secure
Very unsafe—violence or crime was a constant presence
Somewhat unsafe—regularly aware of crime or danger
Very safe—crime and danger were not concerns

text: What matters more in a political leader?

options.label: Articulating a coherent vision that connects all their positions
A track record of actually getting results
Surrounding themselves with experts who craft smart policy
The will to fight for their side against opposition
Having the right position on each individual issue

text: Which identities feel most important to you politically? Rank from most to least:
Which political pitch would resonate most with you?

options.label: "The data shows these three interventions will have the highest impact"
"The other side is destroying everything—we have to stop them"
"Here's my vision for what this country can become and how we get there"
"Here are my 12 detailed policy proposals with cost estimates"
"I've done it before and I'll do it again—check my record"

text: When a political movement develops strong aesthetics (flags, songs, slogans), this is:

options.label: Sometimes necessary but should be viewed skeptically
Natural and healthy—movements need identity
Essential—aesthetic power is political power
A warning sign—substance is being replaced by style
A neutral tool—depends how it's used

text: Which of these political issues matters MOST and LEAST to you personally?
Which of these concerns is MOST and LEAST central to your political thinking?
When evaluating political claims, which matters MOST and LEAST to you?
Which social/moral concern do you prioritize MOST and LEAST?
What aspect of politics engages you MOST and LEAST?
When you think about your political identity, which element is MOST and LEAST defining?
